cards.title: Heart Attack
do_now: Call emergency services immediately
Help patient sit or lie in a comfortable half-sitting position
Loosen tight clothing around chest and neck
Keep patient calm, warm, and still
If alert and not allergic, give aspirin only if advised by emergency operator or local protocol
Start CPR if patient becomes unresponsive and is not breathing normally
do_not: Do not let patient walk or move around
Do not give food, alcohol, or unnecessary medicine
Do not give aspirin if allergic, bleeding, very young, or advised not to take it
Do not give someone else's nitroglycerin
Do not delay calling emergency services
red_flags: Chest pain or pressure lasting more than 10-15 minutes
Pain spreads to arm, jaw, back, neck, or upper belly
Severe sweating, nausea, dizziness, or shortness of breath
Patient collapses, loses consciousness, or stops breathing

cards.title: CPR
do_now: Check if patient is responsive and breathing normally
Call emergency services and ask someone to bring an AED
Place patient flat on their back on a firm surface
Place hands in the centre of the chest
Push hard and fast 5-6cm deep at 100-120 compressions per minute
If trained, give 30 compressions followed by 2 rescue breaths
Use AED as soon as available and follow its voice instructions
do_not: Do not delay CPR if patient is not breathing normally
Do not give rescue breaths if you are not trained or it is unsafe
Do not press on ribs, stomach, or lower end of breastbone
Do not stop until help arrives, AED tells you, patient breathes normally, or you are exhausted
red_flags: Patient is not breathing normally or only gasping

cards.title: Snake Bite
do_now: Call emergency services immediately
Keep patient calm, still, and lying down
Immobilize the bitten limb with a splint or cloth support
Remove rings, watches, and tight clothing near the bite
Keep the bite area below heart level if possible
Note the time of bite and describe the snake from a safe distance if possible
do_not: Do not cut or suck the wound
Do not apply a tourniquet
Do not apply ice or heat
Do not give alcohol or unnecessary medicine
Do not wash the bite if local doctors may use venom residue for identification
Do not try to catch or kill the snake
red_flags: Rapidly spreading swelling or severe pain
Bleeding from gums, nose, urine, or wound

cards.title: Choking
do_now: Ask patient if they can speak, cough, or breathe
If they can cough, encourage coughing
If they cannot breathe or speak, give up to 5 firm back blows
If still choking, give up to 5 abdominal thrusts
Alternate back blows and abdominal thrusts until blockage clears
Call emergency services if choking continues or patient becomes weak
do_not: Do not do blind finger sweeps in the mouth
Do not give water or food
Do not use abdominal thrusts on babies under 1 year
Do not delay emergency help if patient turns blue or collapses
red_flags: Patient cannot speak, cough, cry, or breathe

cards.title: Severe Bleeding
do_now: Call emergency services for heavy bleeding
Press firmly on the wound with clean cloth or dressing
Keep steady pressure until bleeding stops or help arrives
Add more cloth on top if blood soaks through
Raise injured area above heart level if possible
Help patient lie down and keep them warm
do_not: Do not remove the first soaked cloth; add more on top
Do not probe the wound
Do not remove an embedded object
Do not apply a tourniquet unless trained or instructed by emergency services
Do not give food or drink if surgery may be needed
red_flags: Bleeding spurts or does not slow with pressure
Patient becomes pale, cold, weak, or confused
Large wound or amputated body part

cards.title: Burns
do_now: Move patient away from the heat source safely
Cool the burn with cool running water for at least 20 minutes
Remove jewellery or tight clothing near the burn unless stuck
Cover loosely with clean cling film or a clean non-fluffy cloth
Keep patient warm while cooling the burned area
Seek medical help for serious burns
do_not: Do not use ice or very cold water
Do not apply butter, oil, toothpaste, cream, or powder
Do not burst blisters
Do not remove clothing stuck to the skin
Do not wrap the burn tightly
red_flags: Burn on face, hands, feet, genitals, or across a joint
Burn appears white, leathery, black, or charred

cards.title: Seizure
do_now: Clear nearby hard or sharp objects
Protect the head with soft clothing or a cushion
Time how long the seizure lasts
Loosen tight clothing around the neck
After movements stop, turn patient on their side if breathing
Stay with patient until fully awake
do_not: Do not hold patient down
Do not put anything in the mouth
Do not give food, water, or medicine until fully alert
Do not move patient unless they are in danger
red_flags: Patient is injured, pregnant, diabetic, or in water

cards.title: Stroke
do_now: Use FAST: check Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty
Call emergency services immediately if any FAST sign is present
Note the exact time symptoms started or when patient was last normal
Help patient sit or lie safely with head slightly raised
Keep patient calm and monitor breathing
If trained and available, check blood glucose without delaying the emergency call
do_not: Do not give food, water, or tablets
Do not let patient sleep it off
Do not drive patient yourself unless ambulance is unavailable
Do not delay because symptoms improve
red_flags: Weakness or numbness in one arm or one side of body
Sudden severe headache, dizziness, balance loss, or vision trouble

cards.title: Asthma Attack
do_now: Help patient sit upright and stay calm
Help them use their prescribed reliever inhaler
Use a spacer if available
Loosen tight clothing around neck and chest
Call emergency services if inhaler is not helping or symptoms are severe
Start CPR if patient stops breathing
do_not: Do not make patient lie flat
Do not give someone else's inhaler unless directed by emergency services
Do not leave patient alone
Do not delay help if patient cannot speak properly
red_flags: Severe breathlessness or unable to speak full sentences
Blue or grey lips, nails, or earlobes
Patient becomes exhausted, confused, or stops breathing

cards.title: Severe Allergic Reaction
do_now: Call emergency services immediately
Help patient use their prescribed adrenaline/epinephrine auto-injector
Lay patient flat if breathing allows; raise legs if possible
If breathing is difficult, help patient sit upright
Remove trigger if safe, such as brushing off a visible sting
Monitor breathing and prepare for CPR
do_not: Do not make patient stand or walk
Do not give food or drink
Do not delay using prescribed auto-injector during severe symptoms
Do not leave patient alone
Do not ignore symptoms after they seem to improve
red_flags: Swelling of tongue, lips, throat, or face
Symptoms after food, medicine, insect sting, or latex exposure

cards.title: Diabetic Low Blood Sugar
do_now: Help patient sit or rest safely
If awake and able to swallow, give a sugary drink or glucose tablets
Wait 10-15 minutes and check if symptoms improve
Give a longer-lasting snack or meal after recovery
Call emergency services if patient does not improve or becomes unconscious
do_not: Do not give food or drink if patient is unconscious or unable to swallow
Do not give diet drinks because they do not contain sugar
Do not give insulin unless patient is managing it themselves
Do not leave patient alone
red_flags: Sweating, shaking, weakness, or pale clammy skin
Symptoms do not improve after sugar

cards.title: Poisoning
do_now: Call emergency services or poison control immediately
Move patient away from danger if it is safe for you
Find out what was taken, how much, and when
Keep container, label, or sample for medical staff
If chemical is on skin, rinse with running water and remove contaminated clothing
Monitor breathing and consciousness
do_not: Do not make patient vomit
Do not give food, water, milk, or medicine unless told by professionals
Do not smell unknown chemicals closely
Do not wait for symptoms if a dangerous substance was swallowed
Do not enter a gas-filled area without protection
red_flags: Vomiting, stomach pain, burns around mouth, or chemical smell
Empty medicine, alcohol, pesticide, or cleaner containers nearby

cards.title: Heat Stroke
do_now: Call emergency services immediately
Move patient to shade or a cool area
Remove unnecessary outer clothing
Cool rapidly with cold water, wet cloths, fan, or ice packs on neck, armpits, and groin
Keep cooling until help arrives
Monitor breathing and consciousness
do_not: Do not delay cooling while waiting for ambulance
Do not give drinks if patient is confused, unconscious, or vomiting
Do not give alcohol or caffeine
Do not leave patient alone
Do not use fever medicine as the main treatment
red_flags: Very hot body or high temperature
Stopped sweating or heavy sweating with worsening condition
Symptoms after heat exposure or hard exercise

cards.title: Heat Exhaustion
do_now: Move patient to a cool or shaded place
Help patient lie down and raise legs slightly
Remove tight or heavy clothing
Give frequent sips of cool water or oral rehydration drink if fully awake
Cool with wet cloths, sponging, or fanning
Seek medical help if not improving
do_not: Do not give alcohol or caffeine
Do not let patient return to heat or exercise quickly
Do not give fluids if vomiting repeatedly or not fully alert
Do not ignore worsening confusion or fainting
red_flags: Symptoms do not improve after cooling
Very high temperature or hot dry skin

cards.title: Dehydration
do_now: Move patient to a cool place and let them rest
Give small frequent sips of water or oral rehydration solution
Use oral rehydration solution especially after vomiting or diarrhoea
Loosen tight clothing
Monitor urine, dizziness, and alertness
Seek medical help if severe or not improving
do_not: Do not give alcohol
Do not give energy drinks as treatment
Do not force large amounts of fluid quickly
Do not give drink if patient is unconscious or cannot swallow safely
red_flags: Confusion, fainting, extreme weakness, or drowsiness
Sunken eyes, very dry mouth, or cold hands and feet
Baby, elderly person, or pregnant patient with dehydration signs

cards.title: Hypothermia
do_now: Call emergency services for moderate or severe symptoms
Move patient indoors or out of wind and cold
Remove wet clothing carefully
Wrap patient in dry blankets or clothing
Warm the centre of the body first: chest, neck, head, and groin
Give warm sweet drinks only if fully awake and able to swallow
do_not: Do not rub or massage the body
Do not use direct high heat like hot water, heating pad, or fire
Do not give alcohol
Do not give drinks if drowsy or unconscious
Do not move patient roughly
red_flags: Baby or elderly person exposed to cold

cards.title: Drowning
do_now: Call emergency services immediately
Rescue only if it is safe for you; use a rope, stick, or float if possible
Once out of water, check breathing
If not breathing normally, start CPR
If breathing, place patient on side and keep warm
Monitor continuously until help arrives
do_not: Do not enter unsafe water unless trained
Do not assume patient is fine after coughing water
Do not delay CPR to remove water from lungs
Do not give food or drink immediately
Do not leave patient alone
red_flags: Persistent coughing, breathing difficulty, or chest pain after water incident
Confusion or vomiting after drowning event

cards.title: Electric Shock
do_now: Turn off power source before touching patient
Call emergency services for serious shock or high voltage exposure
Use a dry non-metal object to separate patient only if power cannot be turned off
Check breathing and responsiveness
Start CPR if not breathing normally
Cool and cover burns with clean dressing
do_not: Do not touch patient while they are still connected to electricity
Do not use wet hands or metal objects
Do not move patient unnecessarily after a fall
Do not apply ointment or ice to electrical burns
Do not ignore even small-looking entry and exit burns
red_flags: Chest pain, irregular heartbeat, or seizure

cards.title: Broken Bone / Fracture
do_now: Keep injured part still
Support the limb with padding, sling, or splint if available
Apply cold pack wrapped in cloth to reduce swelling
Control bleeding with gentle pressure around the wound
Call emergency services for major injury or deformity
Check circulation below the injury: warmth, color, and feeling
do_not: Do not try to straighten the bone
Do not push exposed bone back in
Do not move patient if neck, back, hip, or leg injury is suspected
Do not give food or drink if surgery may be needed
Do not tie splints too tightly
red_flags: Bone visible or limb looks deformed
Severe pain, swelling, or inability to move
Numbness, blue fingers/toes, or cold limb
Injury to head, neck, spine, hip, or thigh

cards.title: Sprain or Strain
do_now: Rest the injured area
Apply a cold pack wrapped in cloth for 15-20 minutes
Compress with an elastic bandage if available
Elevate the injured part above heart level if possible
Avoid weight-bearing if painful
Seek medical help if severe pain or deformity is present
do_not: Do not apply ice directly to skin
Do not massage strongly in the first 24-48 hours
Do not continue sports or walking through severe pain
Do not wrap bandage so tight that fingers or toes become cold or numb

cards.title: Head Injury
do_now: Ask patient to rest and stay still
Apply a cold pack wrapped in cloth for up to 20 minutes
Check for confusion, vomiting, sleepiness, or worsening headache
Call emergency services if serious symptoms appear
Keep patient under observation
If unconscious but breathing, place on side unless spine injury is suspected
do_not: Do not let patient return to sports or driving immediately
Do not give alcohol or sedatives
Do not move patient if neck or spine injury is suspected
Do not apply ice directly to skin
Do not ignore symptoms that appear later
red_flags: Blood or clear fluid from nose or ears

cards.title: Fainting
do_now: Help patient lie flat on their back
Raise legs slightly if no injury is suspected
Loosen tight clothing
Check breathing and responsiveness
Keep area cool and give fresh air
Call emergency services if patient does not wake quickly or has red flags
do_not: Do not slap or shake patient
Do not give food or drink until fully awake
Do not make patient stand up quickly
Do not ignore fainting with chest pain or breathlessness
red_flags: Chest pain, shortness of breath, or irregular heartbeat
Pregnancy, diabetes, or known heart disease

cards.title: Shock
do_now: Call emergency services immediately
Help patient lie down safely
Raise legs if no injury prevents it
Keep patient warm with a blanket
Control bleeding or treat obvious cause if safe
Monitor breathing and consciousness
do_not: Do not give food or drink
Do not move patient unnecessarily
Do not raise legs if head, chest, spine, hip, or leg injury is suspected
Do not leave patient alone
Do not warm with direct heat
red_flags: Fast breathing or weak rapid pulse
Severe bleeding, burns, allergic reaction, or major injury

cards.title: Nosebleed
do_now: Sit patient upright and lean slightly forward
Pinch the soft part of the nose continuously
Keep pressure for 10-15 minutes
Ask patient to breathe through mouth
Apply cold pack to bridge of nose if available
Seek medical help if bleeding is heavy or does not stop
do_not: Do not tilt head backward
Do not lie flat
Do not stuff tissue deep into the nose
Do not blow or pick the nose after bleeding stops
Do not stop checking time while applying pressure
red_flags: Very heavy bleeding or patient feels faint
Patient is on blood thinners or has bleeding disorder

cards.title: Chemical Burn
do_now: Protect yourself first with gloves or cloth if available
Remove contaminated clothing carefully
Brush off dry chemicals before rinsing if safe
Rinse affected skin or eye with running water for at least 20 minutes
Call emergency services or poison control
Keep chemical container or label for medical staff
do_not: Do not touch chemical with bare hands
Do not apply neutralizing chemicals unless told by professionals
Do not delay rinsing
Do not apply creams, oils, or ointments
Do not reuse contaminated clothing
red_flags: Chemical in eye, mouth, face, genitals, or large area
Severe pain, blistering, or deep burn
Patient becomes dizzy, confused, or unconscious
Industrial acid, alkali, pesticide, or unknown chemical exposure

cards.title: Eye Injury
do_now: Ask patient not to rub the eye
For dust or small particles, rinse gently with clean water
For chemical exposure, flush eye with running water for at least 20 minutes
Cover injured eye loosely with clean dressing or shield
Seek urgent medical help for serious eye injury
Keep both eyes still if object is embedded
do_not: Do not rub or press the eye
Do not remove embedded objects
Do not use eye drops unless prescribed or advised
Do not delay flushing chemical exposure
Do not let patient drive themselves
red_flags: Vision loss, severe pain, or bleeding

cards.title: Animal Bite
do_now: Move patient away from the animal safely
Wash wound under running water with soap if available
Apply gentle pressure with clean cloth for bleeding
Cover with clean dressing
Seek medical help for possible infection, tetanus, or rabies risk
Note animal type and whether it was stray or vaccinated
do_not: Do not ignore small deep puncture wounds
Do not close the wound tightly with tape
Do not apply irritants like chilli, soil, or chemicals
Do not try to catch a dangerous animal
Do not delay care for bites on face, hand, genitals, or near joints
red_flags: Bite from stray dog, bat, monkey, wild animal, or unknown animal
Deep wound, crushed tissue, or heavy bleeding
Bite on face, hand, neck, genitals, or near joint
Redness, swelling, pus, fever, or increasing pain
Patient has weak immunity or diabetes

cards.title: Insect Sting
do_now: Move patient away from insects safely
If sting is visible, scrape it sideways with a card or fingernail
Wash area with soap and water
Apply cold pack wrapped in cloth
Monitor for allergic reaction
Use prescribed auto-injector and call emergency services if severe allergy symptoms appear
do_not: Do not squeeze the sting with fingers or tweezers if avoidable
Do not scratch the area
Do not ignore breathing difficulty or swelling of face/throat
Do not give food or drink during severe allergic reaction
red_flags: Swelling of lips, tongue, throat, or face
Multiple stings or sting inside mouth/throat

cards.title: Suspected Meningitis
do_now: Call emergency services immediately if meningitis is suspected
Keep patient comfortable in a quiet dim area
Check for fever, stiff neck, severe headache, vomiting, and light sensitivity
Check for rash but do not wait for rash to appear
Reassure patient and monitor breathing and consciousness
Keep patient separated from others if infection is suspected
do_not: Do not wait for all symptoms to appear
Do not delay emergency care because rash is absent
Do not give food or drink if patient is drowsy or vomiting
Do not leave patient alone
Do not send patient to school, college, or work
red_flags: Fever with stiff neck or severe headache
Baby with high-pitched cry, poor feeding, floppy body, or bulging soft spot

cards.title: Carbon Monoxide Poisoning
do_now: Move everyone to fresh air immediately if it is safe
Call emergency services and report possible carbon monoxide exposure
Turn off source only if safe to do so
Open doors and windows if safe
Check breathing and start CPR if needed
Seek medical evaluation even if symptoms improve
do_not: Do not re-enter the area until professionals say it is safe
Do not use flames, switches, or appliances if gas leak is also possible
Do not ignore mild headache or dizziness when multiple people are affected
Do not drive yourself if dizzy or confused
red_flags: Headache, dizziness, weakness, nausea, or confusion indoors
Multiple people or pets sick in same place
Chest pain or shortness of breath
Symptoms improve outdoors and return indoors

cards.title: Vomiting and Diarrhoea
do_now: Let patient rest
Give small frequent sips of oral rehydration solution or clean water
Continue breastfeeding for infants if applicable
Use clean hands and safe food/water to prevent spread
Seek medical help if severe, persistent, or signs of dehydration appear
Keep track of urine output and alertness
do_not: Do not give large amounts of fluid at once
Do not give alcohol or very sugary drinks
Do not give anti-diarrhoea medicine to children unless prescribed
Do not ignore blood in stool or vomit
Do not force food if vomiting continues
red_flags: Repeated vomiting and unable to keep fluids down
High fever, severe belly pain, or stiff abdomen
Baby, elderly person, pregnant patient, or weak immunity

cards.title: Panic Attack
do_now: Move patient to a calm and safe place
Speak slowly and reassure them that help is available
Encourage slow breathing without forcing it
Ask about chest pain, fainting, asthma, or known heart disease
Stay with patient until symptoms settle
Seek medical help if symptoms are new, severe, or unclear
do_not: Do not dismiss symptoms as fake
Do not crowd the patient
Do not force breathing into a paper bag
Do not assume it is panic if there is chest pain or breathing trouble
Do not leave patient alone if they feel unsafe
red_flags: Chest pain, fainting, blue lips, or severe breathlessness
First episode or symptoms different from usual
Confusion, seizure, or loss of consciousness
Symptoms after drug use or injury
